interventions.title: EU: New sanctions package includes extended export and import bans, an investment prohibition on Russian mining projects, and other economic sanctions
interventions.description:
On 16 December 2022, the EU adopted Council Regulation (EU) 2022/2474 adding 168 Russian entities to the list of entities to which EU operators are prohibited to export dual-use technologies. Additionally, CN code 8541.60 was also added to the list of affected goods. The regulation is issued in the context of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. It enters into force one day following its publication.

Specifically, the measure adds the 168 entities to Annex IV and adds CN code 8541.60 to Annex VI of Regulation (EU) No 833/2014. The list of dual-use goods includes the following categories: electronics, computers, telecommunication products, sensors and lasers, navigation and avionics, marine equipment, aerospace and propulsion, miscellaneous, special materials, and materials processing.  

In this context, the EU's High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, Josep Borrell, noted: "This will ensure that key chemicals, nerve agents, night-vision and radio-navigation equipment, electronics and IT components that could be used by the Russian war machine cannot be freely traded".  

EU's sanctions on Russia

On 16 December 2022, the EU adopted its ninth sanctions package targeting the Russian Federation for its invasion of Ukraine. The package also includes extended export bans, import bans, and sectoral and other economic restrictions (see related state acts and interventions). 

Update

On 25 February 2023, the EU adopted Council Regulation (EU) 2023/42 prohibiting the transit of EU exports of dual-use goods and technology through or via Russia.

interventions.title: Zimbabwe: Export ban on unprocessed lithium
interventions.description:
On 16 December 2022, the government of Zimbabwe published Statutory Instrument 213/2022 banning the export of unprocessed lithium, with some exceptions allowed by the Ministry of Mines and Mining Development. The measure enters into force immediately. 

Specifically, the measure affects "unbeneficiated" lithium or lithium-bearing ores. The exceptions allowed by the Ministry are made on a written application by parts and are granted in exceptional circumstances. Violation of the ban will result in a fine twice the value of the base mineral or imprisonment of up to 2 years. 

According to Reuters News, Zimbabwe's President Emmerson Mnangagwa previously noted that "the ban would target hordes of artisanal miners, who were drawn by the mineral's high prices and invading abandoned mines to dig for lithium-bearing rocks".

interventions.title: Canada: Investment of CAD 27 million into E3 Lithium's plant
interventions.description:
On 28 November 2022, the government of Canada announced the provision of a CAD 27 million (USD 20.07 million) loan to E3 Lithium, a local lithium company, to fund a lithium production project. The support will be channelled through the Strategic Innovation Fund (SIF) in the form of a conditionally repayable loan.

The investment will support E3 Lithium's CAD 87 million (USD 64.65 million) project involving the construction of a lithium production demonstration plant. On the plant, the company will develop its drilling capacity and test ion exchange technology for lithium mining, as well as enhance process development and lithium hydroxide conversion. 

The government is providing funding through the SIF by disbursing a conditionally repayable contribution covering around 33% of the project's costs. E3 Lithium will have to repay the loan if and when it starts generating gross revenue. 

E3 Lithium is a lithium production company located in the province of Alberta, Canada. 

For information on Canada's Critical Minerals Strategy as well as other recent trade and investment interventions in the critical minerals industry, see related state acts. 

In this context, François-Philippe Champagne, Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry, said: “Canada has everything it needs to build the EVs and batteries that consumers are demanding, and Alberta is key to building that ecosystem and accelerating the low-carbon economy. E3 Lithium’s groundbreaking technology will play an important role in providing large quantities of battery-grade lithium to the auto industry while also creating high-quality jobs for Canadians. This project will help position Canada as a world leader in batteries and will strengthen the critical minerals sector in the Calgary region". 

Strategic Innovation Fund 

Strategic Innovation Fund (SIF) is a government funding program established to make Canada a hub for businesses to invest and grow. The SIF targets industrial and technology sectors and provides funding to large-scale (over CAD 10 million in requested contribution) projects.  The SIF's objectives are boosting Canadian businesses' competitiveness, promoting clean growth and the country's technological edge. Government contributions under SIF are generally repayable but in exceptional cases come in the form of financial grants.

interventions.title: Australia: Announcement of AUD 50 million Critical Minerals Development Program
interventions.description:
On 21 October 2022, the government of Australia announced the Critical Minerals Development Program which would provide AUD 50 million in grants for early and mid-stage critical minerals projects. The program will provide grants over a period of three years. 

The grants can be used for activities such as feasibility studies, engineering design work as well as the building of pilot or demonstration facilities. As a condition for funding, applicants are required to contribute to atleast  half of their total project costs.

The Critical Minerals Development Program is part of the Australian government's commitment to achieve net zero emissions by 2050.

On 17 January 2023, the government of Australia opened the window period for applications until 20 February 2023.

The Critical Minerals Development Program is a part of Australia's Critical Minerals Strategy.

The government of Australia previously provided support to a total of close to AUD 50 million to six firms (please see related stated act).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 150 million to support the construction of a new lithium materials processing plant
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 150 million to Albemarle U.S. Inc to help the company to construct a commercial-scale lithium materials processing plant. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Kings Mountain Lithium Materials Processing Plant” under the battery supply chain segment “Materials Separation & Processing (Cathode Minerals)”. Albemarle will allocate USD 225.9 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “The project objective is to construct a new, commercial-scale U.S.based lithium materials processing plant at Kings Mountain, North Carolina, that uses sustainably extracted spodumene minerals from the site’s lithium mine. This investment would allow Albemarle to process 8,000 tons per day (2.7 million tons per annum) of spodumene ore through a plant designed to produce 1,150-1,200 tons per day (~350,000 tons per annum) of 5.5-6.0% Li2O spodumene concentrate. Such a plant would feed a 50,000 metric ton per year conversion plant to produce battery-grade lithium hydroxide to support domestic manufacturing of the lithium-ion battery cells to power 750,000 electric vehicles per year. Albemarle is finalizing the site selection for the lithium hydroxide conversion plant in the southeastern United States. ”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 57.7 million to support the demonstration of a novel process for manufacturing battery cathode grade lithium hydroxide (LiOH)
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 57.7 million to American Battery Technology Company (ABTC) for the construction of a commercial-scale facility to demonstrate a novel process for manufacturing battery cathode grade lithium hydroxide. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Large-Scale Demonstration of Domestic Manufacturing of Low-Cost and Low-Environmental Impact Battery-Grade Lithium Hydroxide from Unconventional Domestic Sedimentary Resources” under the battery supply chain segment “Materials Separation & Processing (Cathode Minerals)”. ABTC will allocate USD 57.7 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, ABTC and partners “will build, and operate a commercial-scale facility to demonstrate its novel process for manufacturing battery cathode grade lithium hydroxide (LiOH) from unconventional Nevada-based lithium-bearing sedimentary resources. Through this demonstration, the domestic U.S. lithium resource base can be expanded and create a more resilient U.S. battery manufacturing supply chain.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 50 million to support the demonstration of large-scale manufacturing of silicon nanowire anode technology
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 50 million to Amprius to support the demonstration of large-scale manufacturing of silicon nanowire anode technology. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Large scale manufacturing of silicon nanowire anode electrodes by direct gas-to-electrode manufacturing” under the battery supply chain segment “Component Manufacturing (Electrodes)”. Amprius will allocate USD 140.1 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “the objective of this program is to demonstrate the manufacturing of silicon nanowire anode technology at the component and cell level on multi-megawatt-hour (MWh)-scale manufacturing lines that are comparable to those used in multi-gigawatt-hour (GWh) factories. This demonstration will install and operate the first of its kind large scale production lines for Amprius’ ultra-high-energy-density battery technology and provide a bottom-up analysis of the cost factors of the demonstration line and product performance associated with the factory output.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 50 million to support a demonstration project to produce lithium at commercial scale
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 50 million to Lilac Solutions to support the company’s demonstration project to produce lithium at commercially relevant scale using the company’s IX lithium extraction technology. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Unlocking U.S. Lithium Production” under the battery supply chain segment “Materials Separation & Processing (Cathode Minerals)”. Lilac Solutions will allocate USD 129.3 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “Lilac’s project in Fernley, Nevada, will demonstrate the production of lithium at commercially relevant scales (TRL 9) using the company’s IX lithium extraction technology. The technology to be demonstrated has the potential to unlock lithium production from domestic resources that are unviable with current production technologies, and thereby vastly expand domestic lithium supply.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 200 million to support the construction of a lithium hydroxide facility
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 200 million to Piedmont Lithium Inc for the production of lithium hydroxide for the domestic battery and EV market. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Tennessee Lithium” under the battery supply chain segment “Materials Separation & Processing (Cathode Minerals)”. Piedmont will allocate USD 430.4 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “Piedmont is designing Tennessee Lithium to be a world-class lithium hydroxide facility and a large, low-cost contributor to the battery manufacturing supply chain with a sustainability footprint that is superior to incumbent producers.  Located in McMinn County, Tennessee, the project will be sited in an ideal location and uniquely positioned to supply America’s rapidly growing electric vehicle market. At full production, the Tennessee Lithium is expected to produce 30,000 metric tons per year of lithium hydroxide for the domestic battery and EV market, doubling the lithium hydroxide production capacity currently available in the United States.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 100 million for the construction of the world largest silicon anode production facility
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 200 million to Sila Nanotechnologies for the production of lithium-ion anode materials. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Auto Scale Silicon Anode Plant” under the battery supply chain segment “Component Manufacturing (Anode)”. Sila will allocate USD 300 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “funds will support the build-out of a 600,000-square-foot factory housed in Moses Lake, Washington, that will produce breakthrough lithium-ion anode materials. The project is expected to begin production of Sila’s proprietary silicon anode material in 2025, with full production of 20 gigawatt-hour (GWh) equivalent of material at the project’s conclusion in 2026. Sila will build out the factory to full capacity across its 160-acre campus, producing enough materials to power 200,000 electric vehicles (EVs), making Sila’s plant the largest silicon anode production facility in the world.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE awards USD 200 million to support the construction of a battery minerals processing facility
interventions.description:
On 19 October 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) of the United States announced awarding USD 115 million to Talon Nickel (USA) LLC for the construction of a battery minerals processing facility. Funding will be in the form of grants.

Funding is awarded to the project titled “Project “Double Play”: An Advanced Domestic Battery Minerals Processing Facility” under the battery supply chain segment “Materials Separation & Processing (Cathode Materials)”. Talon will allocate USD 318 million as part of the cost-share agreement with DOE.

According to the project description, “Talon Nickel LLC plans to construct a battery minerals processing facility in Mercer County, North Dakota, to support a new domestic cathode supply chain in the United States.  Talon’s proposed facility will process nickel ore from economically viable sources for the domestic manufacture of batteries. Talon has signed a supply agreement with Tesla for 75,000 metric tonnes of nickel in concentrate. The Talon and Tesla supply chain will produce nickel, copper, cobalt and iron in nickel and copper concentrates for multiple battery chemistries. This process improves yield and metal by-product utilization relative to legacy processing of nickel ores.”

Together with the existing award, DOE provided USD 2.8 billion in grants to 21 projects for commercial-scale domestic facilities in 12 states to produce batteries, battery materials and components and demonstrate new approaches. Funding is awarded under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). The funding opportunity announcement was made in May 2022 (see related state acts).

interventions.title: United States of America: DoE provides USD 156 million in funding for the demonstration project of a critical minerals refinery
interventions.description:
On 19 September 2022, the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management (FECM) of the U.S Department of Energy (DOE) issued a funding opportunity announcement for the design, construction, and operation of a Rare Earth Element (REE) Demonstration Facility that extracts and separate rare earth elements (REE) and critical minerals (CM) from sources like mining waste. The total budget allocated for the funding is approximately USD 156 million and funding will be provided through cooperative agreements.

According to the notice of the funding opportunity, funding will be provided in two phases. Eight awards will be made in phase I and one award will be made in phase II. Award recipients are bound by the cost-share proposed in their applications. Specifically, recipients must provide at least 20% of the total project cost as cost share in phase I and 50% of the total project cost for the demonstration project in phase II.

In this context, U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm said: “President Biden’s Bipartisan Infrastructure Law provides America with a historic opportunity to turn legacy waste into the components of clean energy technology in a way that bolsters domestic supply chains and enhances our national security. The demand for clean energy technology continues to grow at a rapid pace and an American-made critical minerals refinery will help generate jobs and increase our competitiveness on the global stage.”

interventions.title: Germany: EIB provides financing for 'Advanced Semiconductor Silicon Wafer Development'
interventions.description:
On 19 September 2022, the European Investment Bank (EIB) and Siltronic AG signed a loan agreement worth EUR 200 million (approx. USD 198 million) for the project 'Advanced Semiconductor Silicon Wafer Development' from Germany. The total cost of the project is estimated at EUR 452 million.

According to the project description issued by the EIB, 'The project relates to the RDI activities for the development of the next generation of hyper pure silicon wafers and ingots for the semiconductor industry as well as the adaptation of the promoter-s state-of-the-art ingot production for most advanced wafer types. The project includes RDI activities in the promoter's RDI locations in Germany as well as the extension of the promoter's production facility in Freiberg, Germany, a cohesion region'.

The objective of the project is defined as follows: 'The project objectives are the development of the next generation of hyper pure silicon wafers and ingots for the semiconductor industry as well as the adaptation of the promoter's state-of-the-art ingot production for the most advanced wafer types'.

A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the investment support granted here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Australia: Government supports two mining firms
interventions.description:
On 16 September 2022, the Ministry of Industry announced a financial grant of AUD 15 million (approx. USD 10 million) awarded to Cobalt Blue Holdings. The grant is aimed at supporting a feasibility study for the beneficiary firm's Broken Hill Cobalt project.

The feasibility study will cover engineering designs and drilling for further resource definition to produce intermediate and battery-ready cobalt products.

Cobalt Blue Holdings operates as a cobalt development and technology firm focused on advancing and developing cobalt mining and refining operations in Australia. The firm is among six firms that have benefited from a series of grants totaling AUD 50 million (approx. USD 36.3 million) by the Ministry of Industry. 

Out of the six firms that have benefited from a series of grants from the government, only two of these grants are GTA reportable. (please see related intervention)

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE announces a funding opportunity to support lithium extraction and conversion from geothermal brines
interventions.description:
On 11 September 2022, the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) of the US Department of Energy (DOE) issued a funding opportunity announcement to support research, development, and demonstration activities to advance the development of a domestic lithium supply chain from geothermal brines. Funding is open to a wide range of applicants including businesses. The total value of the funding is USD 12 million and it will be awarded in the form of cooperative agreements.

The purpose of the funding opportunity announcement is to enable domestic manufacturing of battery-grade lithium hydroxide from geothermal brines and to diversify the supply of lithium hydroxide. Under the FOA, EERE plans to make 3 to 6 awards with individual awards are up to USD 5 million.

A cooperative agreement is a funding mechanism used by the federal government to provide financial assistance to organizations to carry out a project. It is similar to a grant. The main difference between a cooperative agreement and a grant is the level of involvement from the federal agency. In a cooperative agreement, apart from providing funding, the government is actively involved in the project, providing technical assistance and guidance.

Update

On 24 July 2023, DOE announced that it awarded USD 10.9 million for 10 projects that will facilitate innovative technologies to extract battery-grade lithium from geothermal brine sources.

interventions.title: Australia: Government allocates AUD 22 million to Lynas Rare Earths Apatite Leach Circuit
interventions.description:
On 24 August 2022, the Australian government approved a financial grant of AUD 22 million (approx. USD 15.2 million) for Lynas Rare Earths Limited. The purpose of the grant is to implement the Apatite Leach Circuit which would result in the unlocking of additional Rare Earth reserves in the mount ore body.

The beneficiary is an Australian rare-earths mining company. The funding is a part of the Government's Modern Manufacturing Initiative (MMI) in the second round of the program (please see related state acts).

The Australian government first published the grant approval on 15 June 2023.

interventions.title: United States of America: Department of Energy announces a USD 102.1 million loan to support the production of a critical material used in lithium-ion batteries for EVs
interventions.description:
On 27 July 2022, the US Department of Energy (DOE) announced providing a USD 101.2 million loan to Syrah Technologies LLC to help the company expand its graphite-based active anode material (AAM) processing facility.

Funding will be used to finance the construction of the Syrah Vidalia Facility. In this facility, Syrah will produce natural graphite-based AAM to support approximately 2.5 million EVs by 2040. This investment will make Syrah the only vertically integrated, large-scale AAM manufacturer outside of China. AAM is a critical material used in lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicles (EVs) and other clean energy technologies.

In this context, U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm said: “Securing critical materials, such as lithium and graphite, is essential to increasing domestic production of batteries to power the growing number of EVs on our roadways. DOE’s investment in Syrah Vidalia builds on President Biden’s goals to secure our clean transportation future and grow the United States’ electric vehicle and advanced battery manufacturing workforce.”

Funding is provided through the DOE’s Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing (ATVM) program. Authorized by the Energy Independence and Security Act of 2007, ATVM supports the manufacturing of eligible vehicles and qualifying components. The present loan is the first loan from the ATVM Loan Program since 2011.

interventions.title: France: Eramet Group gets funding from the EU's Innovation Fund for recycling lithium-ion batteries
interventions.description:
On 12 July 2022, the European Commission provided a financial grant to Eramet Group to finance its Recycling of Li-ion batteries for electric Vehicle (ReLieVe) project in France. The project was selected as a beneficiary of the EU's Innovation Fund. Although no specific budget per project was announced, the second call's total budget of EUR 1.8 billion (USD 1.8 billion) with a minimum individual budget of EUR 7.5 million (USD 8.5 million) justifies its reportability in the GTA database.   

Specifically, the ReLieve project will construct a lithium-ion batteries recycling plant at the Dunkirk battery cluster for producing and refining black mass, from which one could access critical electrode materials. According to the chemical company BASF, "lithium-ion batteries are composed of metals including lithium, manganese, cobalt, and nickel. Once a battery reaches the end of its useful life, the battery pack can be collected, dismantled, and shredded. The shredded material is then processed to produce so-called “black mass”, which consists of high amounts of lithium, manganese, cobalt, and nickel metals". Eramet Group is a French-based multinational mining and metallurgy company. 

Notably, the ReLieve project is one of the 17 selected large-scale projects (see related state acts).

In this context, EU's Executive Vice-President, Timmermans stated: "Today's grants support innovative businesses across Europe to develop the cutting-edge technologies we need to drive the green transition. (...) Compared to the first disbursement round, the funds available have increased by 60%, enabling us to double the number of projects supported. This is a big boost for the decarbonisation of energy-intensive industry in the European Union". 

The EU's Innovation Fund

Funded by revenues from the auction of emission allowances from the EU’s Emissions Trading System, the Innovation Fund provides financial grants for the demonstration of innovative low-carbon technologies. It is exempted from state aid rules.

interventions.title: United States of America: U.S. Department of Defense provides funding for the establishment of a Heavy Rare Earths (HRE) separation facility
interventions.description:
On 14 June 2022, Lynas Rare Earths Ltd announced that its wholly-owned subsidiary Lynas USA LLC signed a contract for approximately USD120 million with the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) to establish a Heavy Rare Earths (HRE) separation facility in the United States.

Lynas plans to establish the HRE separation facility in the same place as the proposed Light Rare Earth (LRE) separation facility in the State of Texas. Previously, Lynas announced its plans to establish an LRE facility in Texas, and it was half funded by the U.S. government under U.S. DoD Title III, Defense Production Act program (see related state act).

Under the present project, Lynas announced that funding from the government will cover the full cost of plant construction, so the company will not have to put up any capital itself.

In this context, Lynas Rare Earths CEO and Managing Director Amanda Lacaze said: “The development of a U.S. Heavy Rare Earths separation facility is an important part of our accelerated growth plan and we look forward to not only meeting the rare earth needs of the U.S. Government but also reinvigorating the local Rare Earths market. This includes working to develop the Rare Earths supply chain and value added activities." 

Amanda Lacaze further added that “The really important thing here is there is no heavy rare earths separation outside China at present.”

The HRE facility will help the U.S. industry secure access to domestically produced HRE elements that are essential to the development of a supply chain for industries including electric vehicles, wind turbines, fibre optic cables and consumer electronics.  Rare earth elements are also critical to making magnets used in military equipment such as lasers and guidance systems.

To strengthen the United States’ supply chain resilience and manufacturing capacity, and to address supply chain vulnerabilities, the U.S. DoD provides funding to support the production and supply of critical materials and goods. DOD funding can be in various forms. The present contract didn’t specify the type of government support, hence, ‘state-aid, unspecified’ is selected as the intervention type.

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE provides USD 3.1 billion in funding to expand domestic battery manufacturing capabilities
interventions.description:
On 2 May 2022, the Department of Energy (DOE) announced a USD 3.1 billion funding opportunity to support the development of battery manufacturing, processing and recycling technologies. Eligible projects include demonstration projects, construction of commercial-scale facilities, and retrofit or retooling of existing battery material processing facilities. Funding will be in the form of cooperative agreements and grants.

The purpose of the funding opportunity is to ensure that the US has a viable battery materials processing industry. Funds can be used to expand domestic battery manufacturing capability and enhance processing capacity.

Under the funding opportunity, individual awards will vary between USD 50 million and USD 600 million. Award recipients are bound by the cost-share proposed in their applications. Specifically, recipients must provide at least 50% of the total project cost as cost share.

In this context, U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm said: “Positioning the United States front and center in meeting the growing demand for advanced batteries is how we boost our competitiveness and electrify our transportation system. President Biden’s historic investment in battery production and recycling will give our domestic supply chain the jolt it needs to become more secure and less reliant on other nations—strengthening our clean energy economy, creating good paying jobs, and decarbonizing the transportation sector.”

interventions.title: United States of America: Authorization of certain subsidies and public procurement advantages for critical materials in large-capacity batteries following the designation under DPA Title III
interventions.description:
On 6 April 2022, the U.S. Administration issued an authorization determining critical materials in large-capacity batteries as eligible to receive unspecified subsidy payments following the designation of subject products as essential to national defense under the Defense Production Act Title III Section 303. Other support mechanisms are also available under DPA Title III, for details, please see related interventions.

This authorization is based on the government’s determinations that domestic mining and processing of critical materials for the production of large-capacity battery supply chain are essential to the national defense, and without a Presidential action under section 303 of the DPA Title-III, the industry cannot supply these materials in a timely fashion without delay.

Concerning the President’s authorization, the U.S. Department of Defense (DOD) stated that “the United States depends on unreliable foreign sources for many of the strategic and critical materials necessary for the clean energy transition, such as lithium, nickel, cobalt, graphite, and manganese used in large-capacity batteries.  Demand for such materials is projected to increase exponentially as the world transitions to a clean energy economy.” 

DPA Title III authorizes various government incentives to create, expand, or preserve domestic productive capacity for industrial resources, technologies, and materials that are critical for national security. DPA Section 303 incentives can be in various forms including purchases, purchase commitments, subsidy payments, and installation of equipment.

interventions.title: United States of America: Authorization of certain subsidies and public procurement advantages for critical materials in large-capacity batteries following the designation under DPA Title III
interventions.description:
On 6 April 2022, the U.S. Administration authorized various incentives to strengthen and expand the domestic industrial base for critical materials in large-capacity batteries. Such support includes the installation of equipment in industrial facilities. Specifically, the DPA Title III authorizes the president to purchase and install equipment in industrial facilities or to make other types of improvements to these industrial facilities. Additionally, other forms of support mechanisms are available under DPA Title III, for details, please see related interventions.

This follows the government’s determinations that domestic mining and processing of critical materials for the production of large-capacity battery supply chain are essential to the national defense, and without a Presidential action under section 303 of the DPA Title-III, the industry cannot supply these materials in a timely fashion without delay.

According to a statement from the Department of Defense (DoD), “the United States depends on unreliable foreign sources for many of the strategic and critical materials necessary for the clean energy transition, such as lithium, nickel, cobalt, graphite, and manganese used in large-capacity batteries.  Demand for such materials is projected to increase exponentially as the world transitions to a clean energy economy.”

The purpose of the DPA Title III – Expansion of Productive Capacity and Supply – is to ensure that the U.S. has an adequate supply of essential materials and goods deemed critical for the national defense. DPA Title III allows the President to provide financial incentives for domestic private industry to produce and supply these critical materials and goods.

interventions.title: Australia: Critical Minerals Facility gives AUD 1.25 billion loan to Eneabba Refinery Project
interventions.description:
On 4 April 2022, the Australian Critical Minerals Facility approved a state loan for AUD 1.25 billion (USD 939 million) for the Eneabba Rare Earths Refinery in Western Australia. Iluka Resources Limited, an Australian producer of zircon, rutile, as well as rare earths, manages the project. The refinery will produce praseodymium, dysprosium, neodymium and terbium.

The objective of the state loan is to develop the refinery. In this context, the rare earth oxides produced will also serve as inputs to electric vehicles, clean energy technologies, and defence products. As the Australian Prime Minister stated, "Australia’s critical minerals are in demand because they are the key input for everything from mobile phones to fighter jets, not to mention the technologies of the future that haven’t even been realised yet."

The state loan aligns with Australia's Critical Minerals Strategy, which envisions Australia as a "critical minerals powerhouse by 2030." It is also the third state loan granted by the Critical Minerals Facility, an AUD 2 billion loan facility managed by Export Finance Australia (EFA). To view these loans and other government interventions in the critical minerals sector, please, see related state acts.

Update:

In December 2024, the EFA announced additional financing towards the Eneabba Rare Earths Refinery project (please see related stated act).

interventions.title: Switzerland: Export bans against Russia in the field of aviation, space and oil refinery and dual-use goods introduced
interventions.description:
On 4 March 2022, the Swiss Federal Council announced an export ban of certain products to Russia in context to the Russian attack on Ukraine in February 2022 by introducing a revision of the  "Ordinance on measures related to the situation in Ukraine". The export ban entered into force on the same day. 

More specifically, the export of the following products was banned: 

  • Goods and technology that can be used in the aviation and space industries. The Ordinance covers all products falling under tariff chapter 88. 
  • Goods and technology suited for use in oil refining and the oil industry. The Ordinance included products enclosed in 31 6-digits tariff subheadings. 
  • Dual-use goods and technology as listed in Annex 2 of the Swiss Ordinance "On the Export, Import and Transit of Dual-Use Goods, Specific Military Goods and Strategic Goods" from 3 June 2016 (version of 1 January 2022). This Annex 2 includes a total of 6,251 goods enclosed in 929 6-digits tariff subheadings. 

Additionally, services connected with the export of the affected goods to Russia, such as financial and (re)-insurance services as well as repair and inspection services, brokering services and financial assistance are prohibited. 

The Swiss Federal Council has introduced several sanctions over the last weeks, see the related state acts. With the revised Ordinance, Switzerland has also implemented all sanctions introduced by the European Union with the Council Regulation (EU) 2022/328 of 25 February 2022, see the related state act.

interventions.title: EU: Export ban on dual-use, oil refining and aviation goods and technology to Russia
interventions.description:
On 25 February 2022, the European Union adopted Council Regulation (EU) 2022/328 prohibiting the exporting, selling, supplying, or transferring of a series of goods and technology to Russia. The measure enters into force the day following its publication on the official gazette, namely on 26 February 2022. In particular, the regulation bans:

  • The exports of dual-use goods and technology, as enclosed in the Annex of Regulation (EU) 2021/821. 
  • The exports of goods and technology suited for use in oil refining. The affected products were outlined in Annex X of the regulation. A total of 55 products enclosed in 6 6-digits tariff subheadings are affected.
  • The exports of goods and technology suited for use in aviation or the space industry. The affected products were outlined in Annex XI of the regulation. All products under tariff chapter 88 are affected.

The measure forms part of the second sanctions package issued by the EU against Russia in the context of the attack on Ukraine. In this context, the EU's High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, Josep Borrell, noted: "President Putin and his government started a war against an independent, sovereign neighbouring country. The behaviour of the Russian leadership constitutes a major threat to international peace and security. Today, we are replying with the strongest possible restrictive measures". 

It modifies Regulation (EU) 833/2014 which set the sanctions against Russia in the context of the Crimea conflict in 2014.

EU's sanctions on Russia 

On 25 February 2022, the EU instituted its second package of measures targetting the Russian Federation for the recognition of non-government-controlled areas of the Donetsk and Luhansk oblasts of Ukraine as independent entities and the subsequent decision to send Russian troops into these areas. The package also includes restrictions on the provision of financial services to certain Russian entities, and visa procedures for Russian business representatives (see related state acts).

The first package was adopted earlier on 25 February 2022, whilst the third package was adopted on 28 February 2022 (see related state acts). The packages have been closely coordinated with G7 and NATO allies. 

Update

On 8 April 2022, the EU adopted Council Regulation (EU) 2022/576 updating Annex X regarding the list of export goods and technology suited for use in oil refining. The measure removed tariff subheadings 8456.90 and 8543.70 from the list and added 4 other subheadings (see related state act).

interventions.title: United States of America: DOD provides funding to support the development of heavy rare earth separation capacity
interventions.description:
On 22 February 2022, the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) awarded a USD 35 million contract to MP Materials Corp. to help the company design and build a processing facility for heavy rare earth elements (HREE). The project will establish the first processing and separation facility of HREEs in support of defense and commercial applications in the United States.

In this context, Andrew Hunter, who is performing the duties of Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment said: “HREEs are fundamental building blocks of the modern economy, enabling trillions of dollars in global economic development via a wide range of clean energy, information technology, defense, and industrial applications. This effort will enable new domestic industrial capacity, to improve supply chain resilience and to operationalize the policies in Executive Order 14017 on America’s Supply Chains.”

This award is granted as part of DoD’s rare earth supply chain resiliency efforts. To strengthen the United States’ supply chain resilience and manufacturing capacity, and to address supply chain vulnerabilities, the U.S. DOD provides funding to support the production and supply of critical materials and goods. DOD funding can be in various forms. The present award didn’t specify the  type of government support, hence, ‘state-aid, unspecified’ is selected as the intervention type.

interventions.title: Australia: NAIF allocates AUD 140 million to Hastings' Yangibana Rare Earth Minerals Project
interventions.description:
On 2 February 2022, the Australian government-funded Northern Australia Infrastructure Facility (NAIF) approved a loan of AUD 140 million (USD 99.7 million) to Hastings Technology Metals Limited, an Australian rare earths company. 

The objective of the financing is to support the Hastings Yangibana Rare Earths Project. In this context, the project "will involve the development, construction, mining, and processing operations to produce 15,000 tonnes of the valuable Mixed Rare Earth Carbonate each year." It is expected that the project will create over 750 jobs and more than AUD 1.25 billion in public benefit. 

Providing financial funding to the critical minerals industry is an Australian Government priority. To view more information related to funding of critical minerals, please, see related state acts.

Northern Australia Infrastructure Facility (NAIF):
NAIF is an AUD 7 billion lending facility created by the Australian Government to finance infrastructure projects located in northern Australia or with significant impact for that region, and that are of public benefit and engage the indigenous people (please, see related state acts).

The loans provided by NAIF have a lower interest rate than commercial lenders and borrowing tenors of up to 30 years.

interventions.title: Australia: Critical Minerals Facility grants two state loans to spherical graphite projects
interventions.description:
On 2 February 2022, the Critical Minerals Facility, managed by Export Finance Australia, allocated an AUD 56 (~USD 40) million state loan to EcoGraf, an Australian company that produces spherical graphite. 

The objective of the state loan is to build a Battery Anode Material Facility in Western Australia. The facility will produce "high purity graphite products for use in lithium-ion batteries and advanced manufacturing," as spherical graphite is used in electric vehicle batteries. The facility is expected to produce 5,000 tonnes per annum, but the new state loan will assist the facility in producing 20,000 per annum to meet demand in the electric vehicle battery market. 

The project is expected to create 250 jobs during construction and 125 permanent jobs. 

On the same day, the Critical Minerals Facility allocated an AUD 185 (USD 131) million state loan to Renascor Resources for the creation of a new facility to produce spherical graphite. Please, see related intervention.

The state loan for the Battery Anode Material Facility is part of the Australian Government's Critical Mineral Strategy. Please, see related state acts.

interventions.title: Australia: Critical Minerals Facility grants two state loans to spherical graphite projects
interventions.description:
On 2 February 2022, the Critical Minerals Facility, managed by Export Finance Australia, allocated an AUD 185 (~USD 131) million state loan to Rensascor Resources, an Australian company that produces spherical graphite. 

The objective of the state loan is to support the Siviour Battery Anode Material Project in South Australia. The facility will produce Purified Spherical Graphite (PSG) for use in lithium-ion battery anodes that are used in the composition of electric vehicle batteries. 

On the same day, the Critical Minerals Facility allocated an AUD 56 (USD 40) million state loan to EcoGraf for the creation of a new facility to produce spherical graphite. Please, see related intervention. 

The state loan for the Siviour Battery Anode Material Project is part of the Australian Government's Critical Mineral Strategy. Please, see related state acts.

Update

In April 2024, the Australian government confirmed the AUD 185 million loan to Renascor Resources.

interventions.title: China: Government subsidies for listed company Jiangxi Copper Company Limited in year 2022
interventions.description:
In 2022, the Shanghai-listed firm Jiangxi Copper Company Limited (also known as JCCL) disclosed in its stock exchange filings the receipt of approximately USD 44 million of government subsidies.

Jiangxi Copper Company Limited is a Diversified Metals & Mining enterprise based in Guixi City, Jiangxi Province.

In China it is a legal requirement that publicly-listed firms report any subsidies received from government bodies. The stated subsidy amounts refer to a given calendar year. It is possible that this firm received other forms of state aid that have not been declared.

The affected products and sectors have been chosen based on the "main products" and CSRC sector classification reported in the financial data of the firm. The subsidy amounts in USD were computed using the year-average exchange rate to the Chinese Renminbi. The subsidy amount(s) reported here are the cash equivalents as estimated by the recipient firm.

A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects the extent of discrimination against foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the state aid proposed here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Russia: VEB.RF to finance Russian Platinum's project via a joint USD 2.3 billion loan with VTB
interventions.description:
On 30 December 2021, VEB.RF, a national development bank, announced a joint USD 2.3 billion credit line with VTB, a state-owned bank, to support a palladium and platinum production facility. The commitment results from a memorandum of understanding signed by VEB.RF and VTB with Russian Platinum Group. 

Specifically, the funding will support the construction of a mining facility near the Chernogorskoye copper and nickel ore field in the Krasnoyarsk region. The VEB.RF's news release states that the investment will facilitate the creation of "one of the largest palladium and platinum producers in Russia". The facility is expected to be launched in 2024. The total project cost amounts to USD 3 billion. 

Russian Platinum Group is a Russian holding focused on the production of platinum-group metals.

VEB.RF is a national developmental financial institution. It invests, jointly with private sector actors, in the high-tech industry, high-value-added exports, as well as infrastructure upgrading. Financial support from VEB.RF usually comes in the form of trade finance, state loans, and loan guarantees.

interventions.title: United States of America: DOE provides funding for a rare earth separation and processing demonstration plant
interventions.description:
On 5 October 2021, Rare Element Resources (RER) Ltd announced that a consortium of companies led by General Atomics and including RER received financial assistance worth USD 21.9 million from the US Department of Energy (DOE) for the engineering, construction and operation of a rare earth separation and processing demonstration plant to be located in Wyoming. The company didn’t announce the type of funding.

According to the announcement, the award from DOE represents approximately one-half of the total estimated costs for the project. The remainder of the funding will be secured by the project participants.  

In this context, Randall Scott, President and CEO of the Company, said: “We are extremely pleased to have finalized the DoE/EERE financial award. We appreciate the incredible contribution of the General Atomics-led consortium in confirming the DoE/EERE financial support. The funding exemplifies the commitment of the U.S. to secure domestic critical materials production capabilities, and we are appreciative of the diligent work by the DoE/EERE in completing the pre-award process. The planned demonstration plant will produce commercial-grade neodymium/praseodymium (“Nd/Pr”) rare earth high-purity oxide in use in producing high-strength permanent magnets utilizing our proprietary processing and separation technology. These high-strength permanent magnets are a key component in the manufacture of electric vehicles, solar panels and wind turbines, among other technology uses.  We believe our rare earth demonstration project is timely to meet the growing demand of these products.”

interventions.title: Turkiye: Government supports the investment project concerning the production of copper cathode and chemical fertilizers
interventions.description:
On 3 September 2021, the government of Türkiye approved Eti Bakır A.Ş.’s investment project concerning the production of copper cathode and chemical fertilizer in Samsun within the scope of the project-based investment incentive program. The purpose of the measure is to help Eti Bakır minimize the upfront cost burden and accelerate the returns on investments. Under the investment project, the Turkish government agreed to award various tax and social insurance relieves to the company namely corporate tax reduction, social security premium support, and income tax withholding support. The total value of these tax relieves is approximately USD 152 million.

Specifically, the government agreed to provide Eti Bakır 100% reduction from the corporate tax. The corporate tax is 25% in Türkiye. The Turkish government did not set a termination date for the corporate tax reduction support. Instead, the tax reduction support will end once the total amount of taxes not paid reaches 80% of the total investment amount which is USD 151.7 million.

The Turkish government also committed to award approximately TRY 1.7 million (USD 205 thousand) for social security premium support and TRY 1.4 million (USD 169 thousand) for income tax withholding support.

Eti Bakır’s investment project has a total value of TRY 1.572 billion (USD 190 million). Under the investment project, Eti Bakır plans to produce diammonium phosphate with an annual capacity of 250,000 tonnes per year and copper cathode with an annual capacity of 15,000 tonnes per year. The total investment period is 4 years with an option to extend this period up to 6 years.

Apart from tax and social insurance relief measures, the Turkish government agreed to provide customs duty exemption, energy expenditures support, and qualified personnel support. For these support measures, please see related interventions. The government also agreed to provide land allocation, VAT exemption for machinery and equipment to be purchased, and VAT refund for building and construction expenditures. For these support measures, the government didn’t specify the amount of financial support, hence these measures are reported according to the GTA rules.

interventions.title: Canada: EDC supports a commercial transaction carried out by Altius Minerals Corporation in August 2021
interventions.description:
On 9 August 2021, Canada’s export credit agency Export Development Canada (EDC) approved a state loan for Altius Minerals Corporation for a commercial transaction within Canada. For the present transaction, EDC provided the financing amount in the range of CAD 25-50 million. The stated purpose of the transaction is working capital support for a Canadian company.

Given that EDC discloses value ranges for the transactions it finances rather than providing the precise values, the most conservative amount, i.e. CAD 25 million (USD 20 million), was set as the transaction value in our database.

interventions.title: Australia: Government allocates AUD 14.8 million in Lynas Rare Earths
interventions.description:
On 22 July 2021, the Minister for Industry, Science, and Technology announced an AUD 14.8 million (USD 10.9 million) investment in Lynas Rare Earths, an Australian rare-earths mining company. The funding is distributed in the form of a financial grant and is part of the Government's Modern Manufacturing Initiative (MMI).

In this context, the funding will go toward the creation of a new rare earth ore refining process, which is essential for permanent magnet and electric vehicle production. According to the Minister, "The new refining process reduces the need for hazardous chemicals and delivers a significantly higher-grade product for export." In this context, the project is expected to create around 400 job opportunities. 

The MMI is a critical component of the Australian Government's Modern Manufacturing Strategy. One of the core investment sectors is resources technology and critical mineral processing. To view more information on this strategy, please, see related state act.

interventions.title: DR Congo: Government reimposes export ban on copper and cobalt concentrates
interventions.description:
On 28 May 2021, according to consistent news reports, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) issued an order from the Mines Ministry banning the export of copper and cobalt concentrate.

However, as mentioned by a press release from Ivanhoe Mines, a mining operator in Congo, government waivers can revoke the export ban in a case-by-case situation. 

As stated by Ivanhoe Mines’ President and CFO Marna Cloete commented, “The concentrate export ban is not new. However, the rules now recognize that a derogation may be justified for a number of reasons. Kamoa Copper has filed the necessary application materials and we have had constructive discussions with the Minister of Mines on obtaining a derogation for Kamoa-Kakula given current limitations on smelting capacity in-country."

interventions.title: India: Registration requirement imposed on imports of copper and aluminium
interventions.description:
On 31 March 2021, the Ministry of Commerce and Industry (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) through Notification No. 61/2015-2020 amended the import policy for copper and aluminum. The import policy for these goods is "Free" i.e. no prior approval is required for such imports. With the current notification, the policy has been amended to "Free subject to compulsory registration”.

Accordingly, importers of these goods will be required to submit advance information online on the Non-Ferrous Metal Import Monitoring System (NFMIMS). Registration and submission of the information will have to be done between 5 and 60 days of the expected arrival date of the import consignment. The System will provide a registration number that will be valid for 75 days. The number and expiry date of registration will have to be entered into the Bill of Entry for customs clearance.

The NFMIMS system will be effective from all Bills of Entry filed on or after 12 April 2021.

On 10 August 2022, the requirement of an advance registration prior to 5 days of the arrival of the import consignment was withdrawn.

interventions.title: United States of America: U.S. Department of Defense provides funding for the establishment of domestic processing capabilities for light rare earth elements (LREEs)
interventions.description:
On 1 February 2021, the Department of Defense (DOD) announced awarding USD 30.4 million to Lynas Rare Earths Ltd (Lynas), the largest rare earth element mining and processing company outside of China, for the establishment of domestic processing capabilities for light rare earth elements (LREE). Lynas will establish LREE separation capacity in Hondo, Texas. With the completion of the investment, Lynas is expected to produce approximately 25 percent of the worlds’ supply of rare earth element oxides.

LREEs have various applications in different sectors including petroleum refining, glass additives, and magnets used in electric vehicle drivetrain motors and precision-guided munitions.

Funding has been awarded under the Defense Production Act Title III program which provides the President broad authority to ensure the timely availability of essential domestic industrial resources to support national defense and homeland security requirements through the use of highly tailored economic incentives. Specifically, the program is designed to create, maintain, protect, expand, or restore domestic industrial base capabilities.

Update

On 14 June 2022, Lynas signed a follow-on contract with the U.S. DoD to establish a first-of-its-kind commercial Heavy Rare Earths (HRE) separation facility in the United States (see related state act).

interventions.title: United States of America: U.S. Department of Energy invests in advanced processing of rare earth elements and critical minerals for industrial and manufacturing applications
interventions.description:
On 20 January 2021, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Fossil Energy has announced investing USD 28.35 million in advanced processing of rare earth elements and critical minerals for industrial and manufacturing applications. The funding is aimed at creating a sustainable ‘critical minerals’ and ‘rare earth elements’ supply chain that would reduce the country’s risk of supply disruption for essential domestic and military industries, while producing these needed materials for the emerging clean energy technology market.

The federal funding will be provided to cost-shared research development projects that focus on “innovative midstream processing technologies for the purification, recovery, individual separation and reduction to metals of high purity rare earth elements and other critical minerals from coal, coal by-products or alternate non-coal-based feedstock materials.”

In the announcement it is stated that the U.S. is currently importing 80% of its rare earth elements directly from China, with remaining portions indirectly sourced from China through other countries.

Projects will be managed by the National Energy Technology Laboratory.

interventions.title: Poland: EIB provides financing for 'Umicore European Large-Scale Battery Materials'
interventions.description:
On 10 June 2020, the European Investment Bank (EIB) and Umicore signed an agreement worth EUR 125 million (approx. USD 141 million) for the project Umicore European Large-Scale Battery Materials from Poland. The total cost of the project is estimated at EUR 268 million.

According to the project description issued by the EIB, The project consists of the investment for the implementation of a cathode material manufacturing facility. The cathode material will be supplied to battery manufacturers of high tech lithium-ion batteries that are primarily dedicated to the electrical vehicles markets. The investment period covers 2019 - 2021 included..

The objective of the project is defined as following: The aim is to support the promoters investment in the new greenfield cathode material manufacturing facility of the promoter in Poland (Nysa). The project will be carried out during the period between 2019 and 2021, inclusive. The exact timeframe will be verified during the appraisal process..

A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the investment support granted here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Sweden: NIB signs EUR 100 million loan agreement with Boliden Mineral AB to support two investment projects
interventions.description:
On 20 April 2020, the Nordic Investment Bank (NIB) and Boliden Mineral AB signed a loan agreement having a total value of EUR 100 million (approx. USD 108.7 million). The maturity of the loan is 8 years. The loan will support the company ongoing investment programme.

More specifically, the loan will be used to finance the construction of a leaching plant at the Rönnskär copper smelter in Sweden. The new plant will extract metal from waste that is generated by the smelter daily operations that will be transformed into an annual additional output of lead sulphate and copper/zinc sulphate. In addition, the company will also use the economic resources to expand the operations at the Kevitsa mine in Finland (see related intervention).

According to the NIB: “The investment [in the Rönnskär leaching plant] is in line with the company’s strategy to extract as much metal as possible from existing raw material, and will contribute to the smelter’s resource efficiency and competitiveness.

The NIB is an international financing bank and is jointly owned by Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Iceland, Latvia, Lithuania, Norway and Sweden. The bank provides financing to private and public projects mainly located in the eight countries at attractive conditions. The eligibility criteria of NIB financing is that: "All projects financed by NIB should improve competitiveness and/or the environment, in accordance with NIB's mandate and eligibility criteria. Furthermore, outside the membership area, projects financed by NIB should be of mutual interest to the country of the borrower and the member countries."A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the investment support granted here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Indonesia: Nickel ore export ban moved forward by two years
interventions.description:
Indonesian Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources regulation 11/2019 prohibits the exportation of nickel ore with a nickel content of less than 1.7% starting from 1 January 2020. This is an unexpected move by an Indonesian government that moves the export ban forward by more than two years (see related state acts), as the ban was planned to be introduced starting from the 11 January 2022.

The law firm Baker McKenzie mentioned that "The Energy and Mineral Resources Ministry, in charge of mineral and coal in Indonesia, has told reporters that the ban was for the purposes of expediting the smelter building in Indonesia.".

interventions.title: South Africa: IDC provides funding to a mining company
interventions.description:
The Industrial Development Corporation of South Africa (IDC) made publicly available the list of IDC funded businesses in 2019 and in the first quarter of 2020.

One of the beneficiaries has been Kalagadi Manganese (Pty) Ltd, which on 3 October 2019 has received R935 million (approx. USD 61.8 million at the time of formalization of the credit line). Kalagadi Manganese (Pty) Ltd, is a mining company involved in the exploration of Manganese in the Kalahari Basin, Northern Cape.

The Industrial Development Corporation (IDC)

A government-funded South African development finance institution that seeks to promote economic growth and industrial development. To achieve these objectives, the IDC uses different financial instruments such as debt, equity and quasi-equity, guarantees, trade finance and venture capitals, to finance entrepreneurs and companies, helping them to enhance their industrial capabilities.

The funds are limited to companies enclosed in the following sectors: agro-processing and agriculture, chemical products and pharmaceuticals, basic and specialty chemicals, clothing and textiles, heavy and light manufacturing, media and audiovisuals, machinery and equipment, tourism, automotive and transport equipment, industrial infrastructure and basic metals and mining.

interventions.title: Russian Federation: State Development Bank Syndicated Loan for Udokan Copper Mine
interventions.description:
On 10 July 2019, the Russian state development bank VEB.RF and Gazprombank signed a syndicated loan agreement to finance the construction of copper mining facility at Udokan copper mine. The mine will be operated by the Baikalskaya Gornaya Kompaniya (Baikal Mining Company), owned by the Russian billionaire Alisher Usmanov. Udokan copper mining project budget is USD 2.9 billion, with 1.8 financed by the syndicated loan with a maturity term of 12 years. The mine will produce cathode copper and sulphide concentrate.

According to the VEB officials, Udokan is the largest project in Russia financed by the syndicated loan of the VEB "Project Financing Factory". In addition to the state loan, the VEB provides the Company with a government subsidy to compensate for the growth of the basic rate. In addition to the loan and the subsidy, the VEB provides additional funding to cover interest payments on additional financing, should the investment expenses exceed the project budget.

To note, all three major financiers of the project (VEB.RF, Gazprombank and Sberbank) are Russian state entities. 

In December 2020, the Baikal Mining Company was renamed Udokan Copper.

interventions.title: France: EIB grants investment support for Eramet's business activities in the mining and metallurgical industry
interventions.description:
The loan agreement between the European Investment Bank (EIB) and Eramet SA was signed on 25 October 2018 and has a total value of EUR 120 million (approx. USD 136.5 million).

The loan will support the company's project concerning investments in advanced manufacturing and digitisation as well as research and development activities. R&D activities will focus on product development and efficiency improvement of alloy manufacturing and mining operations. The investments in advanced manufacturing will support the company's digital transformation.

In this context, the EIB noted that the aim of the project is to increase the company's: "... knowledge and know-how in the field of metal technology, new materials and alloys as well as to improve the energy efficiency and environmental impact of the promoter's extraction, metallurgical and transformation processes."

According to the EIB, these activities are: "... expected to contribute to increasing the technological competence and leadership of the European metallurgy industry and therefore the sustainable growth and employment in Europe."

Eramet SA is mining and metallurgical company with activities in the area of alloy metals with a particular focus on manganese and nickel. The company has its headquarters in France with operations in more than 20 countries around the world.

A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the investment support granted here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Sweden: NIB signs EUR 110 million loan agreement with Boliden Mineral AB to support its investments in a copper mine and smelter plant
interventions.description:
On 25 January 2018, the Nordic Investment Bank (NIB) and Boliden Mineral AB signed a loan agreement having a total value of EUR 110 million (approx. USD 136.7 million). The loan supports the company's investments related to the Aitik copper mine and a smelter plant, both located in Sweden. The maturity of the loan is 8 years. 

In this context, the CEO and president of the NIB Henrik Normann stated in a press release: "The investments at the Aitik mine and the Rönnskär smelter contribute to eliminating bottlenecks in the production processes, and further strengthen Boliden’s position as a leading company within sustainable mining and metal production. Both projects include deliveries from other companies in the Nordic mining sector"

Boliden Mineral AB has its headquarters in Sweden with operations in Sweden, Finland, Norway and Ireland. The company is a producer of various metals including copper cathodes. According to the company, it produces 350 tonnes of copper cathodes annually making in the third-largest copper cathodes producer in Europe. Copper cathodes are produced from their own mines and smelters related to recycling and are provided mainly to the electrical engineering, construction, electronics and automotive industries. 

The NIB is an international financing bank and is jointly owned by Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Iceland, Latvia, Lithuania, Norway and Sweden. The bank provides financing to private and public projects mainly located in the eight countries at attractive conditions. The eligibility criteria of NIB financing is that: "All projects financed by NIB should improve competitiveness and/or the environment, in accordance with NIB's mandate and eligibility criteria. Furthermore, outside the membership area, projects financed by NIB should be of mutual interest to the country of the borrower and the member countries." 

A state act in the GTA database is assessed solely in terms of the extent to which its implementation affects foreign commercial interests. On this metric, the investment support granted here is discriminatory.

interventions.title: Zimbabwe: Export tax on lithium-bearing ore and dimensional stones
interventions.description:
On 14 March 2018, the government of Zimbabwe published Finance Act 1 of 2018 establishing export taxes on "unbeneficiated" (i.e. unprocessed) lithium and dimensional stones. The measure enters into force retrospectively on 1 January 2018.

Specifically, the Act establishes export taxes as follows:

  • For all export of lithium-bearing ores used in the manufacture of lithium carbonate and electric batteries for automobiles, the export tax is 5%;
  • For uncut dimensional stone (marble or black granite with no or minimal trimming, drilling, cutting or grinding) the export tax is 5%;
  • For cut dimensional stone (marble or black granite sawn into sheets) the export tax is 2.5%.

The export tax on the dimensional stone does not apply if the stones have been smoothed at the edges and polished in the country.

interventions.title: United States of America: The Department of Agriculture provides a loan guarantee to Seven Peaks Mining Company Inc
interventions.description:
On 4 November 2016, the Department of Agriculture approved a loan guarantee worth USD 10 million to Seven Peaks Mining Company Inc located in Lexington, OR, United States of America. The support was granted through the Business and Industry Loans programme.

The Business and Industry Guaranteed Loan Program is aimed at improving the economic health of rural communities by increasing access to business capital through loan guarantees. The program offers loan guarantees to lenders for their loans to rural businesses. The program is administered by USDA Rural Development. The loan guarantee percentage is published annually in a Federal Register notice and the Business and Industry loans approved in Fiscal Year 2021 will receive an 80 percent guarantee.

interventions.title: Canada: EDC supports a commercial transaction carried out by Altius Minerals Corporation in May 2016
interventions.description:
On 3 May 2016, Canada’s export credit agency Export Development Canada (EDC) approved a state loan for Altius Minerals Corporation for a commercial transaction within Canada. For the present transaction, EDC provided the financing amount in the range of CAD 15-25 million. The stated purpose of the transaction is support for working capital/general corporate purposes.

Given that EDC discloses value ranges for the transactions it finances rather than providing the precise values, the most conservative amount, i.e. CAD 15 million (USD 12 million), was set as the transaction value in our database.

interventions.title: Uzbekistan: State support for Almalikski Mining and Metallurgical Plant (12.8 million USD)
interventions.description:
On 29 August 2015 the Cabinet of Ministers of Uzbekistan approved with Decree No. 254 the issuance of credit to Almalikski Mining and Metallurgical Plant. The loan is at an amount equivalent to 12.8 million USD with a maturity of 7 years, a grace period of 3 years and an annual interest rate of 6.00 percent.
 
The credit is related to the contract between Almalikski Mining and Metallurgical Plant and «DAL TEKNIK MAKINA TICARET VE SANAYI AS» (Türkiye). This agreement is for development of working documentation for the technological part, delivery and installation of equipment, construction and installation works at the total amount of 29.0 million USD.
In this regard, the Ministry of Foreign Economic Relations, Investments and Trade of the Republic of Uzbekistan, has developed a feasibility study for the investment project "Expansion of a cement plant in Jizzakh* region". The deadline for finalisation of this project is September 2016. Decree No. 254 also approved the technical and economic parameters of this particular project-related feasibility study.
 
The GTA includes state guarantees and other financial incentives that are likely to affect the restructuring and performance of firms facing international competition, whether from imports, in export markets, and from foreign subsidiaries.

interventions.title: Uganda: Export ban on several mineral ores
interventions.description:
In February 2015, according to news reports, the government of Uganda imposed a ban on the export of iron ore, tungsten (wolfram), coltan, copper and phosphates ores. 

As the exact date of the action was not announced, following the GTA's conservative approach the last date of the month was selected as the inception date (i.e. 28 February 2025). 

Notably, in 2012, the government of Uganda adopted a similar ban on iron ore (see related state act).

Update

On 29 August 2015, according to news reports, Uganda partially lifted the export ban. The iron and copper ores export ban remains in force.

interventions.title: Canada: EDC supports a commercial transaction carried out by Capstone Mining Corp in January 2015
interventions.description:
On 15 January 2015, Canada’s export credit agency Export Development Canada (EDC) approved a state loan for Capstone Mining Corp for a commercial transaction within Canada. For the present transaction, EDC provided the financing amount in the range of CAD 50-100 million. The stated purpose of the transaction is general corporate purposes.

Given that EDC discloses value ranges for the transactions it finances rather than providing the precise values, the most conservative amount, i.e. CAD 50 million (USD 41 million), was set as the transaction value in our database.
