interventions.title: Brazil: FINEP signs a USD 10.52 million loan with Bemisa - Brasil Exploração Mineral SA in December 2014
interventions.description:
On 22 December 2014, FINEP approved a BRL 27.96 million (approx. USD 10.52 million) loan in favor of Bemisa - Brasil Exploração Mineral SA. The beneficiary is a Brazilian company operating in the following economic activity: mining and metal exploitation.

FINEP is a public financial institution attached to the Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (MCTI in Portuguese) of Brazil. It provides reimbursable and non-reimbursable resources to Brazilian research institutions and companies to support them throughout the stages of the scientific and technological development cycle for the industrial sector.

interventions.title: China: Suspension of solar polysilicon imports for the processing trade
interventions.description:
On 14 August 2014, the Ministry of Commerce and the General Administration of Customs jointly issued the "Announcement of Suspending the Application of the Importation of Solar Polysilicon for Processing Trade" (MOFCOM Announcement '2014' No.58).
 
According to the announcement, MOFCOM will not approve the importation of solar polysilicon (HS codes: 2804.61.90.12, 2804.61.90.13, 2804.61.90.92, 2804.61.90.93) under processing trade. The contracts approved by MOFCOM before 1 September 2014 are allowed to be enforced within its terms.
 
Since China has applied anti-dumping duties to solar polysilicon producers from the EU, South Korea and the US in 2014, there has been an import surge of solar polysilicon under the catogery of "processing trade". The termrefers to the business activity of importing all or part of the raw and auxiliary materials, parts and components, accessories, and packaging materials from abroad in bond, and re-exporting the finished products after processing or assembly by enterprises within the mainland.
 
With the implementation of this measure, the circumvention of anti-dumping duties on solar polysilicon via this route will no longer be possible.

interventions.title: Indonesia: Conditional export tax exemptions for unrefined mineral exporters
interventions.description:
On 25 July 2014, the Indonesian Ministry of Finance amended its regulation on export tax for unrefined metal minerals (cf. Related Measures). With the amendment 153/PMK.011/2014, the export duty has been cut for export companies that are constructing smelters. The duty exemptions depend on the stages of construction.
As noted in paragraph 4A(3) of the amendment, the 'stages construction progress referred to in paragraph 2 are as follows: 

  • Stage I: the progress of the construction up to 7.5% including the seriousness guarantee 'i.e. a deposit of 5% meant to show the company's seriousness in constructing the smelter';
  • Stage II: the progress of the construction of more than 7.5% up to 30%;
  • Stage III: the progress of the construction over 30%.' (own translation)

According to the appendix of regulation 153/PMK.011/2014, exporters in stage I will pay an export duty of 7.5%, in stage II 5% and the export duty will be completely ceased for exporters in the third stage.
As cited by the Jakarta Post ("Lower export duties come with close monitoring", 5.8.2014), the Director General for mineral and coal at the Energy and Mineral Resources Ministry R. Syukhar announced that "every six months, development must reach at least 60 percent of its target. If the target is missed, we will halt the companies' exports".
The amendment is another measure in a long line of incentives to increase the local-based refining process of minerals by the Indonesian government (cf. Related Measures).
The new rules came into force one week after the enactment, i.e. 1 August 2014.

interventions.title: Russian Federation: State subsidies to the rare earth industry
interventions.description:
With Decree 42/21.1.2014 the Russian government approved state subsidies to the Russian rare earth industry with a total amount of RUB 735.2 million (US$ 21 million) for the period 2014-2016.
 
According to the official document, the state support recipients are entitled to credit interest rates reimbursements. The purpose of the state support is to increase the competitiveness of the local rare earth miners and to meet the rare earth demand of the Russian defence industry.

interventions.title: Indonesia: Increased export tax on certain mineral ores
interventions.description:
On 11 January 2014, the Ministry of Finance issued Regulation MOF 6/2014 increasing the export duty levels for certain types of raw minerals. Ten different minerals are affected by the tax measure, a complete list can be found in the annex of the regulation (see sources).
 
The export tax for all mineral ores will increase from 20 percent in January 2014 to 60 percent in July 2016 before exports will be banned altogether in 2017. The exception to this process is copper which starts at 25 percent.
 
The measure partly overrules the regulation 75/2012 (see related measures).

interventions.title: Indonesia: Export provisions on mineral ores tightened
interventions.description:
On 11 January 2014, Indonesia released Regulation 1/2014 to further control exports of mineral ores. The new regulation amends Regulation 7/2012 (see related measures) and stipulates the processing and refining criterias. In sum, the export of processed mineral ores is getting more difficult due to the higher criteria of the new regulation.
 
This regulation is an application of the Mining Law (see related measures) and is designed to support the in-country processing and refinement of the minerals to increase value added in the sector.
 
For the holders of valid mining permits and contracts of work, semi-processed metals in the form of copper, iron, ilmenite, titanium, lead, zinc and manganese concentrates can still be exported until 12 January 2017. Nevertheless these export are subject to an additional tax (see related measure).
 
A complete list of processing and refining criteria can be found in the sources. Furthermore, a link to the old regulation is provided to allow for a detailed comparison.

interventions.title: Vietnam: Increased import duties on certain mineral resources
interventions.description:
On 25 April 2013, the Vietnamise Ministry of Finance issued Circular no. 44/2013/TT-BTC increasing import duties on the following mineral resources:
 
Increase of duty from 30 to 40 per cent on:
- Apatite ore (excluding fine grain type less than 15 mm)
- Manganese ore and extracted ore (including iron-bearing manganese ore or extracted ore with manganese content exceeding 20 per cent in dry volume)
- Copper, lead, tin, and other titan ores (excluding ilmenite reclaimable ore, titan ash, artificial and synthetic rutile).
 
Increase of duty from 25 to 30 per cent on:
- Lime rock stone 
 
The amendment entered into force on 9 June 2013.

interventions.title: Indonesia: Export tax on a variety of products including mineral ores
interventions.description:
On 18 May 2012, the Finance Ministry introduced an export tax of up to 40 percent on 65 products (Regulation 29/2012 and Regulation 75/2012), including 14 formerly banned mineral ores and an additional 51 products. Thegovernment intends to promote the production and export of 'high value'finished goods instead of exports of raw materials. 
On 6 May 2012, the Ministry of Energy and Mining (according to Regulation No. 7 of 2012) introduced an export ban on 14 mineral ores including copper, gold, nickel, tin, silver, lead, zinc, chromium, platinum, bauxite, iron ore and manganese (related measure).

On 28 May 2012, the Ministry of Trade issued regulation 33/M-DAG/PER/5/2012 implementing the regulation 75/2012 mentioned above.
In January 2014, this measure was partially updated with a further intervention (see related measure).

interventions.title: Guatemala: Alterations in the exporting rules for waste and scrap of metals and other minerals
interventions.description: On 18 January 2012, the government of Guatemala published Government Agreement 32-2012 (in Spanish: Acuerdo Gubernativo 32-2012). Amongst the key changes introduced by this Government Agreement are (for the first time) the establishment of an export quota system for waste and scrap metal and other minerals, which will be managed via the issuance of export licenses. One such license involved an export quota of 30.000 metric tons of waste and scrap ingots of iron or steel, cast iron scrap, alloy steel, stainless steel, iron or tin-plated steel, turnings, shavings, chips and cuts.A second license was for the export of 2.000.500 metric tons of waste and articles of nickel, zinc, tin, tungsten, molybdenum, tantalum, magnesium, cobalt, bismuth, cadmium, titanium, zirconium, antimony, magnesium, beryllium, chromium , germanium, vanadium, gallium, hafnium, niobium, indium, rhenium, thallium and cermet.

interventions.title: China: Evidence for the manipulation of rare earth prices
interventions.description:
In October 2011, numerous and consistent press articles reported on two incidences of potential price manipulation of the price of Chinese rare earths exports.
 
On 18 October 2011, the largest Chinese rare earth mining company Baotou announced a halt to its production. The company said it would pause production for a month "balancing supply and demand." Baotou's share in Chinese rare earth extraction is estimated to lie around 40 percent.
 
A second string of press reports hints at the informal enforcement of "minimum export prices" through the Chinese customs authorities. Apparently, export contracts had to be rewritten in order to qualify for international shipment.

interventions.title: Japan: State aid for projects aiming at the reduction of the use of rare earth minerals
interventions.description: On 11 May 2011 the permanent delegation of Japan announced to the WTO their intention to provide financial aid for capital investment projects aiming at reducing the use of rare earth minerals. The financial aid will be granted to 160 projects by the Japanese Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry. The overall budget is 33 billion yens (US$407.7 million.

interventions.title: China: Increase of more than 1000% of an adjusted rare earth tax
interventions.description:
On 25 March 2011, the Chinese Ministry of Finance (MOF) and the Chinese State Administration of Taxation (SAT) jointly announce a circular to imposed an adjusted Rare Earth Tax from 1 April 2011.
 
In accordance with this Circular, the tax rate of mined light rare earths will become RMB 60 yuan ($9.1) per ton, while that of medium and heavy rare earths is RMB 30 yuan ($4.5) per ton. Before this Circular adopted, the taxes of rare earth minerals were levied under the category of ordinary non-ferrous metals ores, with tax rates between 0.5 and 3 yuan ($0.07 and $0.4) per ton cubic meter.
 
The increase in the adjusted rare earth tax will lead to heavier duties on rare earth producers, both Chinese and foreign, as well as for buyers. It is expected that rare earth prices on world markets may rise again. Should the application of this measure be asymmetric, in particular if local governments taxChinese firms operating in their jurisdiction less, then there is the potential for this measure to be discriminatory.

interventions.title: China: Neodymium rare earth export tariff increase in 2011
interventions.description:
From 1 January 2011, the Neodymium ( Nd, a rare earth element, used for lasers, violet colors in glass and ceramics, ceramic capacitors) will be levied with a 25 percent export tariff. In 2010, the Nd export tariff was 15 percent. 
 
In accordance with the Notice of the Customs Tariff Commission of the State Council on Implementation of Customs Tariff 2011 'Shuiweihui 2010 No.26', which is released on the website of Ministry of Finance on 14 December 2010, Neodymium rare earth element will be levied with higher export duty in the year of 2011 than in 2010.
 
In 2010, the rare earths, Yttrium(Y), Europium (Eu), Dysprosium (Dy), Terbium (Tb), Scandium (Sc), had an export tariff of 25 per cent. In accordance with the Notice, these tariff rates remain on the same level (25 percent) in 2011. In 2010, for other rare earth elements including Neodymium (Nd) export tariff rate was at 15 percent. 
According to the Notice, the tariff rate will remain at 25 per cent and also other elements are levied with a tariff at 15 percent. Dysprosium-Ferrum Alloy (an alloy with rare earth element Dysprosium) and Neodymium-Ferrum Alloy ((an alloy with rare earth element Dysprosium) were under 20 percent export tariff rate in 2010. In accordance with the Notice,these product tariff rates remain at 20 percent in 2011.

interventions.title: Canada: EDC supports a commercial transaction carried out by Lundin Mining Corporation in September 2010
interventions.description:
On 1 September 2010, Canada’s export credit agency Export Development Canada (EDC) approved a state loan for Lundin Mining Corporation for a commercial transaction within Canada. For the present transaction, EDC provided the financing amount in the range of CAD 25-50 million. The stated purpose of the transaction is general corporate purposes (incl. operations in sweden, portugal, spain and ireland, as well as exploration activities).

Given that EDC discloses value ranges for the transactions it finances rather than providing the precise values, the most conservative amount, i.e. CAD 25 million (USD 24 million), was set as the transaction value in our database.

interventions.title: Afghanistan: Introduced royalty tax on the exports of minerals
interventions.description:
The Law of 'Minerals' of 14 February 2010 regulates the extraction of minerals in the Islamic Republic of Afghanistan for exportation.
It sets a royalty tax of 15% on the commercial invoice value of all minerals except for lapis. With respect to lapis, it sets a royalty tax of 15%, depending on the grade of the estimated stone value.

interventions.title: Russia: Introduction of export duty on nickel
interventions.description:
The Government Decree #1017 from 16 December 2009 has introduced an export duty of 5% of the customs value on nickel (HS 7502).
By the Government decree 312 from 25 April 2011 the export tariff on nickel (HS 7502) was introduced from 27 May 2011. The export tariff depends on the average price of the nickel at London Metal Exchange and is calculated according to the formula attached to the decree.

From 5 March 2012 the export tax for nikel is 1245.5US dollars per tonne (Government Decree 74 from 3 February 2012).
 
From 5 June 2012 the export tax for nikel is 1447.6US dollars per tonne (Government Decree 431 from 4 May 2012).

interventions.title: China: Increased import VAT on several minerals
interventions.description: On 19 December 2008, the Ministry of Finance issued Notice No. 99 of 2008, to increase the import VAT on 82 tariff lines, from 13% to 17%. The new policy will come into effect on 1 January 2009. No further information is stated.
